mnemonics.title: Function Call Steps - SAVE-RESTORE
mnemonics.content: PUSH {registers you modify} → Perform function operations → POP {same registers in reverse} → Return. Remember: Always restore what you save!

mnemonics.title: Leaf vs Non-Leaf Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: LEAF = Last Exit After Function - doesn't call others. NON-LEAF = Needs LR saved (Nesting requires LR on stack).

mnemonics.title: Register Usage - 0-3-4-12 Rule
mnemonics.content: R0-R3: Pass first 3 args, return value in R0. R4-R11: Callee-saved (preserve these). R12: Caller-saved scratch.

mnemonics.title: Stack Operations Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: PUSH = SP decrements THEN stores. POP = Retrieves THEN SP increments. Full Descending = Grows toward lower addresses.

acronyms.usage: Defines how functions pass parameters, return values, and use registers in ARM programs
Instruction for subroutine calls that saves return address in LR
Holds return address during function calls in ARM
Register that holds address of next instruction to execute; modifying PC transfers control
Points to top of stack; used for storing/retrieving data during function calls
Provides stable reference for accessing function's stack frame parameters and locals
Instruction to push multiple registers onto stack in ARM
Instruction to pop multiple registers from stack in ARM

visualTips.visualization: Draw stack as a vertical column. ARM uses 'Full Descending' - imagine a stack of plates being placed on a table, each new plate goes BELOW the previous one (lower address). SP points to the 'top' which is actually the lowest address used.
Draw a timeline: CALLER → [BL saves PC+4 in LR] → EXECUTE FUNCTION → [POP PC restores saved address] → CALLER. For nested calls, show how LR gets saved to stack and re-saved for each nested call.
Draw 4 boxes for R0-R3 (argument/return), 4 boxes for R4-R7 (callee-saved), 4 boxes for R8-R11 (callee-saved), and R12 in middle (scratch). Color-code: green (caller-saved), red (callee-saved).
Draw stack growing downward. Show saved LR at higher address, then saved registers, then local variables if any. FP points to a fixed location for easy access to all these.

analogies.analogy: Imagine calling a company (caller), your call is transferred to department (function). The receptionist notes where to return your call (LR saved). When department finishes, they transfer back to receptionist who reconnects you (return). Nested calls are like supervisor → manager → employee → back up the chain.

Think of callee-saved registers (R4-R11) as office equipment that must stay in your office (function). If you need to use them, you bring your own (save/restore), not take from common pool. Caller-saved registers (R0-R3) are like shared markers - anyone can use them.

Each function gets a locker (stack frame) with a number (Frame Pointer). You store your belongings (saved registers, locals) there. When function ends, you take your stuff and leave the locker empty for the next person.

analogies.concept: BL Instruction as Placing a Bookmark
analogies.analogy: BL is like placing a bookmark in a book before jumping to a different chapter. The bookmark (LR) remembers where you were. When done, you return to the bookmarked page (MOV PC, LR).
